speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Goldie Weisz. We are in Miami, Florida. And we're interviewing in English. Tape 1. Would you please introduce yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Igor Kling. I live in North Miami, Florida. I'm 70 years old. So I recently celebrated my 70th birthday. I am married for over 40 years to my wife, Lena.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, at birth, my name was Ignatzik Klinghoffer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: K-L-I-N-G-H-O-F-F-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that name changed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Somewhere in the late '50s I changed my name from Klinghoffer to Kling. And my first name, I changed to Igor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any nicknames as a boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my nickname was Igio, which was for Ignatzik.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who gave you that name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess my parents. That's how they called me when I was  a youngster.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is the date of your birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born January 30, 1925.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Boryslaw, Poland. This is the southeastern part of Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell Boryslaw, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, B-O-R-Y-S-L-A-W.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you living in a house or an apartment in Boryslaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a house. We had a house, which by the way, is still in existence.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: At home, we spoke Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you still speak Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you speak it often?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't speak too often. But I speak very well Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have friends who speak Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do have who speak Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us a little bit about Boryslaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Boryslaw was a city with many oil wells. And that was the main industry. As a matter of fact, the oil wells from Boryslaw supported the larger part of the neighboring town Drohobycz which had refineries for the oil from the wells in Boryslaw. It was refined into gas and other byproducts in Drohobycz in the refineries. And it was a small industrial town, and I would say, quite on the upper level comparing with other small towns and Poland because of oil, which you could see, even at that time, oil was important. And there was very nice cultural life in Boryslaw, I remembered. There was a visiting theater groups, opera. And there was sporting events of all kinds. And we had a private schools and private high schools. And it was a quite well-to-do town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to private schools?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I went to private schools. Just a private prep school and also to private, at that time, was called gymnasium. It was the equivalent of a high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father was a CEO of a drilling equipment factory-- drilling equipment for the oil industry, for the petroleum industry. And also there was a bank-- the Polish name was Bank [NON-ENGLISH]. And he was the director of that bank. But that position was voluntary. He was doing this to help small merchants and people who needed loans for business primarily.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you describe what life was like before the occupation in Boryslaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: For me, as a young boy, as a student, life was maybe a little bit better than the average everywhere else. The fact that we were quite well-to-do, we could afford domestic help. And summertimes, we spent six weeks up to two months in the resorts nearby. And we had good clothes and sporting equipment. And it was a very nice life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you also go to Hebrew school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I did not go to Hebrew school. I had a private Hebrew teacher preparing me for bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had a bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did have a bar mitzvah. As a matter of fact, the big event was that somebody was filming it. And I think that was one or two people, professional people, in Boryslaw who at that time had a movie camera.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of a kid were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was, well, I was average height-- average build. I was interested in sports. Schools-- I did quite well in school. And I did very well with Latin, French. We also had the French nanny who taught me French in addition to the French that I was taking up in school. So languages came very easy to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other languages do you speak?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, of course, English-- I hope its quite well-- French, German, Polish, Russian. I understand other Slavic languages, but just understand. But these languages I could speak.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yiddish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and Yiddish, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Yiddish spoken at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish was not spoken at home. But there were quite a few expressions that my parents exchanged between themselves. And I picked them up. And then in concentration camp, since I was among Jews all the time, I picked up Yiddish. And then German I picked up by living four years after the war in Salzburg, Austria. And then I was studying at Vienna University for a year. So I learned German there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you want to be as a little boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know if that was my idea at that time because I was too young. But my parents wanted me to be an engineer possibly to work in the petroleum industry with my father, or after my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Jewish community life like in Boryslaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a Jewish community. But there was not necessarily a Jewish community life. There were no special Jewish-sponsored events. Very few, if any. But there was a feeling of being Jews-- Jewish. Of course, we observed holidays, Passover, Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur. And then, of course, there was a group of Jews who were more a little bit Orthodox. We were what is called today conservative. We observed the holidays. We had no bread at home for Passover-- there was a different set of dishes. But we were, I would say, moderate religious-- kosher home we had though.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you observed Shabbat.

speaker: interviewee
text: To a degree. That was a Friday night maybe a little bit more festive than the other days. But we didn't observe strictly in following Shabbat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a sister a few years older who survived the war. And she died 15 years ago in America.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Helena.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your experience with anti-Semitism before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was anti-Semitism. But I personally did not experience any. There was a second anti-Semitism-- the fact that my sister was older, as I said, who graduated gymnasium high school who wanted to study-- wanted to go to medical school. So she couldn't be accepted because of being Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she had to go to Vienna University. And then subsequently, when Hitler invaded Austria, then she inscribed into the university in Brussels. But then the war broke out. And she never continued. So in that sense, we did experience anti-Semitism. Otherwise, on a personal basis, I did not experience. Did you know what was happening to the Jews in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I knew when they started coming to Poland en masse after '36, '37, then I learned from the newspapers. But we were so far away from that, it really-- and I was a young boy. So I don't know how much really I was involved in it or even interested in that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents make any preparations prior to the occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, none whatsoever. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: For what reason? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no reason, because the fact is that-- well, first I have to mention that when the war broke out in '39, the Germans came in. They occupied our part for about a week or 10 days. But then according to their agreement between the Russians and German, they left our part. And the Russians came in. And then occupied our territory for two years until 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was no reason for us to-- my father was not politically involved in any way, although we had a large house-- in order to be able to stay in this house and maintain it, we had to take three or four families to live with us so we could stay in our house. Otherwise, we supplement my father's ability to through his previous position was retained by the Russians. They gave him some different titles.

speaker: interviewee
text: Even the pay was not sufficient. So we had enough, I don't know, some jewelry, other things that we supplement our income. So life was bearable. I went to school to continue my education with my friends. So I would say I was as close to normal as one can imagine, maybe not too many luxuries. But of course, in retrospect we say it was a fantastic life with what happened after June, July '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the first signs of danger?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no signs of danger. There was just, all of a sudden, when the war broke out, within a few days, the Germans occupied our town. And the next day, they were ready to let Ukraine have a free hand. They were starting to rob and to walk into the homes. They picked up quite a few men. There was a headquarters of the NKVD, which was the Russian secret police, which was abandoned by the Russians who fled, like I said, after the outbreak of this war. And so there were telltale signs of their doings. There were beatings. There was blood in many cellars. So they took the Jews, and they made them clean up. As a matter of fact, they took advantage of them. They beat them up. Those Jews, they just grabbed them from the street. And in certain spots, there was sulfuric acid spilled, which the Russians had left. And they made them sit in that subsequently. There was a lot of very unpleasant things. A few  were shot. But that was just like the beginning three days after they had marched in. And then subsequently, the hell broke loose.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was the Ukrainians who did these things, mostly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Ukrainians were the ones who were leading the Germans. And then the Germans were beating up somebody. So the Ukrainians felt a free hand to do the same thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your house looted that way also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Slightly, slightly. Because they came in. They grabbed my father. And they left. And then two days later, there was like nothing ever happened. It was normal life that we thought was normal. But then the restrictions started coming every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean that they grabbed your father? You mean, they took him out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, from the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did they do with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: But he was the only man. They took him to the headquarters of the NKVD.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did they do to him there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they just roughed him up a little bit and then made them clean some stables and some other things. I don't know where they kept him for overnight and just let him loose the next day with other people. I think one or two men were shot for whatever reason or no reason. I mean, they never had any reason to shoot somebody. But they would just start.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then how did your life change after the occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there was a drastic change, drastic change. Maybe a few days later, a few weeks later, they came to our house. And whenever they saw furniture, paintings, piano-- we had a big, grand piano. And everything that they wanted, they took out. Then one day, there was an official notice that all the radios have to be deposited in a place. So we had to stay for hours and hours in a line to deposit a radio, because we had one or two radios like everybody  had. Then the furs we had to give. So we gave the furs. And we were so afraid of everything that I might never forget, my mother had little house shoes, which were trimmed with the little rabbit fur or something. So she ripped off this and threw it in the bundle to give it and not to be accused of hiding furs. Obviously, they needed those furs, the Germans, for their uniforms-- to make uniforms for those who fought on the Russian front. The winter was coming. It was like, I don't know, December, January or so, '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in the house when they came in to ransack it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, oh, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was so frightened. Nobody uttered a word even. There was just a picture of despair on everybody's face. Everybody was so frightened. They were sort of thinking. When they left, and then everybody left unharmed. There were four  people-- my father, my mother, my sister and I. So we were thinking, well,  thank God for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other experiences did you have during the occupation? Did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, I worked-- worked every day. I had various odd jobs. One day there was a strong rain-- there was a small river-- Tysmienica going through the town. And it was small. It was, but still was overflowing. So we went with the shovels in hands, we had to save whatever we can from the river. Then subsequently, I worked in a big auto repair depot. Now, in retrospect, the fact that I worked there, and then when we were taken two years later to a different concentration camp in Plaszow I put my occupation as auto mechanic. And that profession next to my name put me eventually on Schindler's list, and I guess, saved my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened then? What happened after you  were working as an auto mechanic? What else did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, subsequently, there was a factory-- one day, and it was in 1942, there was so-called action. And it was in August '42. And in the meantime, the abilities and knowledge of the industry, my father was among very few a handful of men who worked in the office of the headquarters of the petroleum industry [NON-ENGLISH]. And command of this whole office was a German by the name of Berthold Beitz. There is a whole story about him I could tell you later maybe. And my father worked actually for him among a few other men. In August 1942, they took away my mother and my sister. And I was working out of the-- I call it out of the house. And then he found out that there was a round up. They took everybody to the railroad station where they have freight cars. And they load them up. And loaded them up. And they were about to leave that evening the following day. And my father found out-- he and another man who was an engineer. Engelberg was his name. His daughter was also taken away. So they both begged this Beitz if you could do something. So finally, they convinced him. So he got into the car. And he went to the railroad station. When you saw the movie there was a scene where Schindler knocks on the doors of the freight cars and looks for the men of the name Stern. Now, visualize in reality that is what happened in 1942. This Beitz with a Gestapo officer knocking-- there was a noise, because of screams, and yelling-- knocks on every freight car. And he yells the name Klinghoffer, Klinghoffer, Engelberg. Klinghoffer was the name of my mother and my mother and Engelberg. And finally, one of the car doors open. And there was my mother and my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they grabbed my sister. As she was pulling my mother, an SS man shoved the door and pushed my mother back. And that was the last of my mother and my sister. Then he went to another car. And he picked up this girl, Engelberg. And the two of them survived. They were taken to a home and subsequently they went through all kinds of different difficulties. But that was at the time. He saved her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, a footnote to that is that this Berthold Beitz survived the war. And he became the chairman of the board of the Krupp  Werke. This is the largest industrial chain of factories in Germany. And he survived, obviously. And as a matter of fact, he came to New York. He was awarded a humanitarian award. And there was a dinner for him. We went. It was a tense moment. But still, even though he was compensated subsequently, but it's not important. But the fact is that he did save my sister. He gave her another 20, 25 years to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what happened to your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I know what happened to the whole transport. On the way to Majdanek, to the gas chambers. Nobody came back from that transport. It was a few thousand people that were taken-- men, women, children. They didn't discriminate. They just took everybody, because there were quite a few survivors who came after the war from different places. From that transport, not one person came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were in the ghetto in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we were in  the ghetto, yeah. Subsequently, after that, we went to the ghetto. And then when they were cleaning up and taking all the actions and transporting modules, we were down to about, I think, 1,000 who were working for the-- well, they use to give us R with the lapel shield, which means rustung, armament in German. And then we were put in this old, abandoned-- there was a military barracks from World War I. And subsequently there were stables. By the time they put us there, it was abandoned. So we were about 1,000 people we were there. And that was until the end-- until they took out everybody from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were you there with?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was still there with my father and my sister, who had married the physician. And she was working in a local ambulatory for the inmates of that particular [INAUDIBLE]. And my father was still working till the end until they took us out. And all of us, we were taken by the same transport in June '44 to Plaszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Taken to Plaszow. And what did you do in Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, first of all, I have to mention that in Plaszow after four days, they took my father to a different camp in Wieliczka which is not far from Plaszow. But I never saw him after that again. And my sister stayed in Plaszow. And then subsequently she went to Auschwitz, Stutthof, and different camps. And she survived. And I was in Plaszow. And from Plaszow, I went through Gross-Rosen to Brunnlitz. That's Schindler's factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do in Plaszow? Anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I worked in an auto repair depot.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they sent you to Gross-Rosen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they sent me to Brunnlitz. But it was via Gross-Rosen. Actually, they sent us to Auschwitz. But at that time there was so many transports, so many trains were coming to Auschwitz, that they had no room. They couldn't accommodate our train. So we stayed there for like a day or so. And then they switched us over to Gross-Rosen. At Gross-Rosen, they unloaded us. And then they picked the ones who were on Schindler's List from Gross-Rosen and sent us to Brunnlitz, which is Czechoslovakia where Schindler--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get on that list?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the biggest puzzle of my life. Nobody knows, not even I. I and a friend of mine who still lives in Israel who I visited last year, we sensed there were a lot of people who had clout because they were from Krakow. And they worked for Schindler before to such a point that I had never knew at that time who was Schindler. I didn't know anything about  the list. I didn't know anything about anything at all until I found myself in Czechoslovakia. And then I realized that there is a Schindler. Well, on the way, I found out. But I had no idea. Now, my own thinking is the reason that I found myself on that list was because next to my name was the profession auto mechanic. And since none of these people were any professional adults, they figured maybe they need some real ones. And I was not a real mechanic either. I had a little knowledge of repairing trucks and automobiles. But maybe he and-- and a friend of mine, who was listed as an auto mechanic's helper. So he and I, we just found ourselves in that factory in Czechoslovakia. And that's how we survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to Brunnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Transport?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the freight cars, yes, from Gross-Rosen, they loaded us up.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the whole transport went to Brunnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's the only transport. Oh, no, no, no. The transport from Plaszow that went to Gross-Rosen. No, there were a few thousand people that were left. Some of them went to Auschwitz. The women went to Auschwitz. The men went to Gross-Rosen. And from those people who went to Gross-Rosen, I don't know. I thought it was 700. But I understand there were 800 men who went to Brunnlitz. And then, subsequently, the 300 women from Auschwitz came too. But as a matter of fact, a friend of mine-- he happens to live in the same building-- he went with me all the time until Gross-Rosen. And then he was left in Gross-Rosen. But he was not on the list. Subsequently, he went to, I don't know, Mauthausen and different camps. No, only a select group from Gross-Rosen were sent to Brunnlitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in Brunnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: About six and a half, seven months-- about seven months-- until the last day in May-- until the 8th, 9th of May.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you liberated from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we were liberated, yes, by the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then started a little odyssey on my part. I wanted to go back to my hometown, Boryslaw, with this friend of mine who was also from my town. And we were hitchhiking and walking. And all kinds of modes of transportation were used, sometimes with the Russian soldiers, until we got to about 50, 60 miles-- 40 miles from our town. At that time, there was a border set up. That was Poland. And east of that city Przemysl, there was a Ukraine-- called in Russia Ukraine. And they set up a tremendous camp. And they were catching all Russian collaborators who worked for the Vlasov Army. Vlasov was a Russian general who deserted the Russians. He abandoned the Russians. He went to work for the Germans. He fought with Germans. And he recruited a quite considerable army who fought on the German side against Russians. So they were catching them. And they set them up in this camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So our misfortune was that we fell into this camp. And we couldn't explain to anybody because nobody believed us that we were for real. We were so desperate, we were almost ready to commit suicide after having gotten through that hell of the four years, and the Germans, and everything else. But then, we realized there was group of non-Jewish Poles who were coming from different camps. Since they registered as Polish nationals, they were let go after small processing. So I didn't shave for a few days. And my friend put some bandages over his eyes. Anyway, so we mixed in with a group of those boys. And we registered with fictitious Polish-sounding names. So they let us out. And that's how we went on the western part-- to Poland and subsequently to Krakow. And from there, a whole new life started.
